items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: When a stress shifts an equilibrium, does the system fully undo the change?
items.correct: No — Le Chatelier's principle says the system PARTIALLY opposes the change; equilibrium shifts to a new position that minimises but doesn't eliminate the disturbance.
items.misconception: The system shifts to completely cancel the change and return concentrations to their original values.
items.random_wrong: The system shifts to amplify the change because reactions always proceed in the disturbed direction.
items.why: Students treat Le Chatelier as a perfect homeostat, missing that the new equilibrium reflects the new conditions and only partially counters the disturbance.

items.q: Which metal will displace copper from copper sulfate?
items.why: It's Iron. “Gold” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Sodium is stored under oil because:
items.correct: it reacts vigorously with water and oxygen
items.why: The answer examiners want is: it reacts vigorously with water and oxygen. “it dissolves in air” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In the order K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Cu, Ag, Au — what position is iron (counting from K=1)?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
